title: SELECT with WHERE and ORDER BY

description.task: Practice SELECT queries using various WHERE conditions and ORDER BY clauses to retrieve and sort data.
description.starterCode:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  emp_id     INT PRIMARY KEY,
  name       VARCHAR(60),
  department VARCHAR(40),
  salary     DECIMAL(10,2),
  hire_date  DATE,
  active     BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE
);

INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,  'Alice',   'Engineering', 95000, '2020-01-15', TRUE),
  (2,  'Bob',     'Marketing',   65000, '2021-03-20', TRUE),
  (3,  'Carol',   'Engineering', 88000, '2019-06-10', TRUE),
  (4,  'Dave',    'HR',          55000, '2022-08-01', TRUE),
  (5,  'Eve',     'Engineering',105000, '2018-04-05', TRUE),
  (6,  'Frank',   'Marketing',   70000, '2023-01-12', TRUE),
  (7,  'Grace',   'HR',          58000, '2020-11-30', FALSE),
  (8,  'Henry',   'Engineering', 91000, '2021-07-22', TRUE);

-- Query 1: All active Engineering employees, ordered by salary desc
SELECT name, salary, hire_date
FROM employees
WHERE department = 'Engineering' AND active = TRUE
ORDER BY salary DESC;

-- Query 2: Employees hired between 2020 and 2022
SELECT name, department, hire_date
FROM employees
WHERE hire_date BETWEEN '2020-01-01' AND '2022-12-31'
ORDER BY hire_date;

-- Query 3: Employees with salary BETWEEN 60000 and 90000
SELECT name, salary FROM employees
WHERE salary BETWEEN 60000 AND 90000
ORDER BY salary;
instructions: Run all INSERT and all three query statements.
Query 1: should return Eve(105000), Alice(95000), Henry(91000), Carol(88000).
Write a Query 4: find all inactive employees (active = FALSE).
description.hint: BETWEEN is inclusive on both ends. AND/OR can combine multiple conditions. Wrap OR conditions in parentheses when mixed with AND to avoid precedence issues.
description.expectedOutput:
Q1: Eve>Alice>Henry>Carol (Engineering)
Q2: Alice(2020), Grace(2020), Carol... wait — filtered by 2020-2022
Q3: Bob(65k), Frank(70k), Carol(88k)

title: SELECT with LIKE, IN, and NULL

description.task: Use LIKE for pattern matching, IN for multi-value filtering, and IS NULL / IS NOT NULL for null checks.
description.starterCode:
CREATE TABLE customers (
  customer_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  name        VARCHAR(60),
  email       VARCHAR(80),
  country     VARCHAR(40),
  phone       VARCHAR(25)
);

INSERT INTO customers VALUES
  (1,  'Alice Smith',    'alice@mail.com',  'USA',   '+1-555-0101'),
  (2,  'Bob Johnson',   'bob@mail.com',    'UK',    '+44-7700-900001'),
  (3,  'Carol Smith',   'carol@mail.com',  'USA',   NULL),          -- no phone
  (4,  'Dave Williams', 'dave@mail.com',   'Canada','+1-416-555-0202'),
  (5,  'Eve Brown',     'eve@mail.com',    'USA',   '+1-555-0303'),
  (6,  'Frank Miller',  'frank@mail.com',  'AU',    NULL),          -- no phone
  (7,  'Grace Wilson',  'grace@gmail.com', 'UK',    '+44-7700-900100'),
  (8,  'Henry Moore',   'henry@mail.com',  'Canada','+1-416-555-0404');

-- Query 1: Customers whose name contains 'Smith'
SELECT name, email FROM customers WHERE name LIKE '%Smith%';

-- Query 2: Customers from USA, UK, or Canada (using IN)
SELECT name, country FROM customers
WHERE country IN ('USA','UK','Canada')
ORDER BY country, name;

-- Query 3: Customers with no phone number
SELECT name, country FROM customers WHERE phone IS NULL;

-- Query 4: Gmail users (email LIKE pattern)
SELECT name, email FROM customers WHERE email LIKE '%@gmail.com';
instructions: Run all INSERT and all four queries.
Q1: Alice Smith and Carol Smith.
Write a Q5: customers WITH a phone number (IS NOT NULL) from USA.
description.hint: IS NULL and IS NOT NULL are the only correct way to check for NULL — WHERE phone = NULL doesn't work because NULL != NULL. LIKE '_' matches any single character, '%' matches zero or more.
description.expectedOutput:
Q1: Alice Smith, Carol Smith
Q2: 7 rows (all except Frank/AU)
Q3: Carol(USA), Frank(AU)
Q4: Grace Wilson

description.task: Use DISTINCT to eliminate duplicates, LIMIT to cap results, and OFFSET for simple pagination.
description.starterCode:
CREATE TABLE orders (
  order_id   INT PRIMARY KEY,
  customer   VARCHAR(50),
  product    VARCHAR(60),
  category   VARCHAR(40),
  amount     DECIMAL(8,2),
  order_date DATE
);

INSERT INTO orders VALUES
  (1,  'Alice', 'Laptop',      'Electronics', 999.99, '2026-01-05'),
  (2,  'Bob',   'Mouse',       'Electronics',  19.99, '2026-01-06'),
  (3,  'Alice', 'Keyboard',    'Electronics',  59.99, '2026-01-07'),
  (4,  'Carol', 'Desk',        'Furniture',   250.00, '2026-01-08'),
  (5,  'Bob',   'Monitor',     'Electronics', 299.99, '2026-01-09'),
  (6,  'Dave',  'Notebook',    'Stationery',    5.99, '2026-01-10'),
  (7,  'Alice', 'Office Chair','Furniture',   175.00, '2026-01-11'),
  (8,  'Carol', 'Headphones',  'Electronics',  79.99, '2026-01-12'),
  (9,  'Eve',   'Laptop',      'Electronics', 999.99, '2026-01-13'),
  (10, 'Dave',  'Mouse',       'Electronics',  19.99, '2026-01-14');

-- DISTINCT: unique customers who have placed orders
SELECT DISTINCT customer FROM orders ORDER BY customer;

-- DISTINCT: unique product categories
SELECT DISTINCT category FROM orders ORDER BY category;

-- LIMIT: top 3 most expensive orders
SELECT order_id, customer, product, amount
FROM orders
ORDER BY amount DESC
LIMIT 3;

-- Pagination: page 2, 3 items per page (offset 3 means skip first page)
SELECT order_id, customer, product
FROM orders
ORDER BY order_id
LIMIT 3 OFFSET 3;
instructions: DISTINCT customer: Alice, Bob, Carol, Dave, Eve (5 unique customers).
LIMIT 3 by amount: Laptop(999.99), Laptop(999.99), Monitor(299.99).
Add a comment on how OFFSET pagination performs on large tables.
description.hint: OFFSET pagination is simple but slow on large tables because the DB must skip N rows each time. For high performance, use cursor-based pagination (WHERE id > last_seen_id LIMIT N).
description.expectedOutput:
Distinct customers: 5
Distinct categories: Electronics, Furniture, Stationery
Top 3: 2x Laptop + Monitor
Page 2: orders 4,5,6
